reason: Both answers hit the core judo moves well: change the axis, weaponize the incumbent's breadth as a liability, target an underserved segment, and get ahead of the RFP/bake-off criteria. They're comparable in structure and clarity. B edges ahead on decision-relevance: it adds two things A lacks — a concrete POC/demo tactic (hands-on trial with the buyer's real workload vs. a feature demo) and, importantly, an honest caveat that positioning judo can't fix a genuine feature gap in your chosen segment, with a suggested way to diagnose which problem you actually have (win/loss interviews). That caveat matters for correctness/usefulness since it prevents the asker from over-applying the advice. B is a bit longer but the extra content is substantive, not padding.
